title: Lesson 395
transcript: We've reached almost the end of this really long section in domain number four. So pat yourself on the back because we've gotten there at least. Remember this first section in domain number four is again aligned completely with the exam outline. And we've been talking about architecture, right? So we've been talking about the OSI model. We've been talking about transport protocols and architectures and performance monitoring and so on. Related to this, there's a really big need for organizations to monitor and manage what the network is doing on a regular basis. And again, the message of all of this is going to be that security needs to be involved in this because obviously there are some focuses that require security to be involved and so on, all right? So here's first of all, a definition of what this means. Although if you focus on the words monitoring and management, tells you exactly what we're talking about here, right? We need to look at the network And what it's providing as far as services and traffic and the ability to reach your customers and so on, on a regular basis to ensure. Obviously, that first of all, the network and its services are always available. They're performing as expected and with priority. proper security and reliability of all of those services that our stakeholders, including our clients and customers, are really relying on. So this is important to monitor on an ongoing basis. And specifically, what's mentioned in the exam outline related to this are what you see here. First of all, these are self-explanatory. There's no need for us to spend a lot of time on it because it tells you exactly what we're talking about here. Like for example, network observability, the ability to be able to look at the network and how it's functioning to gain insights. On the network itself, its ability to provide the services that obviously our stakeholders are requiring from us on a regular basis. That requires us to monitor the network and look at it and manage it, of course, on a regular basis. The second one, again, self-explanatory, traffic flow, traffic shaping, controlling the movement and the services that are being delivered through the network in ways that, again, get better. and enforce policies, even those related to security, for example. So if we need to prioritize something over something else, we should be able to manage the network so that we can prioritize things based on needs and requirements and Client focus and so on, right, on an ongoing basis. That's what traffic flow or traffic shaping is all about. Exactly what those words are telling us. Capacity management, again, self-explanatory here. Being able to obviously monitor the network and plan. accordingly, not only to ensure that we have the proper resources for what we need today, but also to meet, for example, future demands. So planning the capacity of your network and its services. to be able to meet not only today's needs, but tomorrow's as well. Anticipating, obviously very important as far as network performance and management is concerned. And fault detection and handling, This relates to being able to react and correct things as they're happening, right? So being able to diagnose issues as they are happening in real time so that it doesn't affect the quality, the integrity, and the service that we're providing to stakeholders on a regular basis. This is pretty important. And this could be part of your incident management capabilities, but obviously here we're focused specifically on the network itself. and the ability to be able to deal with any sort of problems that occur in real time so that we can address them in real time as well. And then, you know, monitoring and management include some other things that are also self-explanatory. Like obviously monitoring the network for performance. Again, related to some of those metrics that we talked about before. Monitoring to ensure that confidentiality, integrity, availability, authenticity, non-repudiation aspects of the network and its services are always being taken care of. to the level that is required. Changing things from a configuration perspective, making sure that we do it using a rigorous process of change management, that includes and has security be part of it becomes important as well. Then of course, we talked about the importance of other things, but in this case, related specifically to the network itself, log management. and being able to interpret those logs and react in real time. Alerting and notification for things like incidents and so on. Maybe having a security operations center that obviously looks at the services and the network itself to be able to not only analyze but react effectively in real time, very important part of the services that we provide. Obviously, giving assurance to stakeholders including management, and others that the network is providing the services that it needs to, but also in a secure way. The message again, these are all self-explanatory. but it needs to include security aspects. The whole message of this entire course really is that security needs to be part of anything that represents value to not only the organization, and its third party connections that it has, but obviously to clients and customers and shareholders. In other words, stakeholders, internal and external as well. Security needs to be part of everything including the monitoring and management of networks and its services.
